column4: Rao, Zheng, and Yang 2023 Table 2 CDTRF-G formulation transcribed on the table's stated volume-fraction basis
column5: The five printed component fractions sum to exactly 100 percent. The source presents CDTRF-G as a FACE G-targeted surrogate formulation, not a compositional assay of FACE G. Component suppliers, lots, purities, preparation order, mixing temperature, mixing pressure, and treatment of volume contraction are absent. The DIB label is retained verbatim and is not generalized to an arbitrary commercial diisobutylene mixture. The FACE G hydrocarbon-class targets discussed in the text use a molar basis and are not laundered into the table's volume-fraction composition

column4: Rao, Zheng, and Yang 2023 Table 2 reported calculated research octane number for CDTRF-G
column5: The paper says surrogate properties were calculated using linear mixing, but its surrounding derivation discusses component molar fractions while Table 2 labels the formulation by volume fraction. No CFR-engine measurement, test standard, repeat count, dispersion, or confidence metadata is reported for this value. Table 2 prints RON 94 while Table 7 prints RON 93.9 for the identical five component fractions; the two values remain separate claims and are neither averaged nor reinterpreted as uncertainty

column4: Rao, Zheng, and Yang 2023 Table 7 Fuel 2 reported calculated research octane number at the same composition as Table 2 CDTRF-G
column5: Table 7 prints RON 93.9 for 23.75 percent isooctane, 19 percent n-heptane, 42.75 percent toluene, 9.5 percent DIB, and 5 percent cyclohexane by volume. This conflicts at the displayed precision with Table 2's RON 94 for the same composition. No CFR-engine measurement, test standard, repeat count, dispersion, or confidence metadata is reported; both printed values remain separate and no preferred value is inferred
